Hey, what's going on everyone? Welcome. This is the video explanation of what the hybrid model is. This is just an explanation of how it works, the concepts behind it, etc. And then underneath it, this you should see one or several videos giving any examples of different gyms and different fitness professionals who are able to use this with their business to make a lot more money. So it's a combination of getting more customers more profitably and also transforming the existing business model to be a bigger profit center. So let's dive in. So the gym model of the future hybrid the secret of combining online and in-person training. So to start I just want to explain the only ground rule that we operate on as a business and that we assume that you operate with too. All right. A business owner wins by making the same prospect more valuable to his business than to that of his competition. All right, because ultimately if you can do that, that means you can now spend everyone, you can now serve and just crush, right? And so by extension the gyms that create the most profit per customer will dominate their respective marketplace. Because the more profitable your core offering, the more you can turn around and spend on marketing to attract more customers, thereby helping more people, the more you can spend on serving those same customers because you have more profit left over to over deliver, the more invested they will be, the better results they will get, and the more your clients will talk about your business, and the more money you ultimately make, right? All by structuring your core offer as the biggest profit center in your business. And so I want to show you how some of our gym lords have taken this business truth and applied it to a new model hybrid and used it to massively scale their profit and decrease overhead. Before I dive in, let me just tell you how this all started and why we even decided to make this shift. So about six months ago, I started noticing a trend, right? One, lead cost had quadrupled across all markets in the past 24 months, and that's just like across industries, across markets, it's just the cost per eyeballs has quadrupled. And this is specifically in Facebook since that's where a majority of people are getting their leads, all right? So quadrupled in the last 24 months. Showup rates have dropped by 40% in the last six months. More gym closes past year than opened, and big box gyms are offering group training now at 25% of the price and have better everything, right? And the profit per location has dropped to 10% or less, making it a grind to survive. There we go. And whenever there's a conference events like this, it sets the stage for a massive shift in the marketplace. And that's kind of when I had my own shit moment of like, oh gosh, we need to change things because ultimately my fate is tied with the gym industry, right? If you don't make money, I don't make money. And so my incentive is 100% aligned with how much profit I can make you so that you could pay us, right? Like that's how this works. Like we make people money so that they can pay us. If they don't make money, they don't pay us and then everyone's sad and you know, no one likes to be sad, right? And so I had to keep working and digging and trying to figure out how we can rework the model to make more money, right? And so finally it came to me. I was watching some of our gyms get seduced by the promises of internet business, right? You've seen these gurus all over the place. You know, everyone makes money down here, right? The thing is, I knew that no program was yielding 90% plus results, success rates for purely online. And I defined results as recouping in a small investment in the first eight weeks, right? That's how we, you know, for us. That's how we see this for our program. And that's what we know on the gym side. But I knew no one was doing that, right? And they weren't even getting 30% success rates for that matter. And I know that because I bought all the programs, right? Because I'm going to do anything I can to serve gyms. All right. And so I wondered, is there a way that we can combine the two into a super hybrid gym model, right? Online plus in person equals hybrid gym. Because online wise, there's a ton of pros, right? There's high margin. It's easy to fulfill. It's perceived as higher value to end users. You can get higher prices for it because there's less commodization or price comparison. There's lower churn because it's one-on-one attention. The cons, and there's only really only one big one for online, is that it's really hard to get clients in an online environment because it's very low trust, right? It's low trust, especially with cold traffic. If you're doing organic, you know what I mean? And you're posting and you're getting people who've commented on your stuff. And you're going that way like, you can get clients, but you're not going to get anything in a significant amount of volume unless you have a massive following, right? But most people don't have that. And so I have to make something that, like I said, 90% plus are going to succeed with. And so that is not a viable option for me. So if cold traffic is the only way that I can do this, and cold traffic for online is very low trust and difficult to do, right? And that's why none of the other programs out there really worked, right? That's why I don't have any massive businesses that are doing this because they haven't produced massive results, right? While on the flip side, right? In brick and mortar, there are pros there too, right? And this is the business that we're in, right? It's super easy to sell people into in-person training in a gym. It's very easy to get clients. The cons are that it's hard to fulfill, meaning it costs a lot. It's difficult in terms of time, right? It's perceived as lower value. It's more commoditized. There's lower margins overall. There's lots of effort to scale delivery. Having 100 DFTs versus 500 DFTs is a 90 day difference in terms of staffing, in terms of the different systems you have, you need to have front desk, you need to have sales managers, you need to have retention managers, you know, challenge managers. There's lots of different things you need to add to the business to keep it flowing so you don't have to be there, right? And so it takes effort and operational know-how to scale it in-person, right? And once you have that, it still takes effort to retain people, right? Because you don't have one-on-one service, and so you have to put all these other systems in place, which we've already figured out, but it still takes effort, right? And so we decided to run a test, right, of this new kind of concept that we were working on in two different groups, right? And we had one that was purely online and one that was this hybrid model. And a lot of times we try crazy things, right? And a lot of times they don't work, and that's part of testing things. If you know it's going to work, then it's not a test, right? But one of them did work, right? And it worked big time. And that's when hybrid was born, right? And so this is the hybrid way. So pros are, you get all the pros and you kind of wipe out the cons, right? So pros for hybrid is it's easy to sell, it's easy to get clients, it's easy to fulfill, it's perceived as high value, it's high margin, there's a wider radius than hyperlocal. So you can actually serve more people. There's no cap on the revenue or profit per location. So you don't need to open multiple locations to order to make more money. You can make one location, make multiple, multiple, multiple millions of dollars in profit or revenue, because you're no longer limited by your square footage, right? And on top of that, you can even downsize your facility to reduce rent even further and drive more profit, right? Now the cons are that you still have to pay rent of some sort because you need to meet with people in person in order to facilitate the whole process and not to make it scalable so that everyone can sell this very, very easily, right? And so I believe in this so much that I'm betting my entire business on it. And so before I even dive into that next thing, I literally have ended legacy, which is our back end program that we have for scaling gyms, and I ended launch, which is a front end program, and put everyone into hybrid because I believe that this is the future. This is the future of the business, and I believe in so much that I combine everything into this core model, okay? So here's how it works, right? In the beginning, it looks the same, right? Suzie sees a Facebook ad, she gets in her car, she sews up to your gym, right? All of this point is all the same for a normal gym, right? Except now when she leaves, she goes home, and then when she does her workouts, she doesn't actually do them at your gym. She does them on the treadmill, she walks outside, or she goes to Planet Fitness or whatever, some $10 month gym, so that she can get her sweat on to her preferences because it depends on what she wants, and she might not want to do CrossFit for the rest of her life, right? And I'm like, we're in no way affiliated, right? But I'm saying like, in any way. But the point is, is that like, what her desire is might change, right? And so we need to be cognizant of that. And so that actually allows us to keep someone longer because it allows us to change the fulfillment based on their needs, all right? And so on top of that, she checks in every 14 days at your facility, so you're still going to use it for in-person check-ins, builds rapport, you also upsell product when you're there. And then you do a post-check three times a week via text. Very, very simple, all right? That's it. That is it. Literally. It's crazy. And so what is happening is it provides the best of both worlds, of online and in-person. And so just imagine that you have the exact same revenue you have right now, except the only thing you have to pay is rent and nothing more. How much more time would you get back? How much less stress would you be, right? How much more money would you make? A lot, right? And so that might be like, cool, Alex, but like, show me the numbers, right? So here's the math. This is all I ever care about. It's the math. How it breaks down, right? And so this is the current model that we run, all right? And this is what, you know, gym launches made zillions and zillions of dollars off of, and the gyms that we represent, you're over a billion dollars in sales, okay? Combined. And so this is the current model. And it works well, all right? So we'll show it to you. So you spend a thousand bucks on leads, you get 100 leads, 70 schedule, 35 show, 20 close at 500, right? Profit model typically for, you know, gym weights you 20%. You have 20 people at 500 times 20% is $2,000 of profit, right? And so that means that you spent, you made $2,000 of profit, divided by your 100 leads is 20 bucks a lead, which is pretty much where you max out on the acquisition side, right? That's what it looks like. Now, hybrid model. Spend a thousand, get 100 leads, 70 schedule, 35 show, everything's the same, right? 20 close at 500, profit is 90%, and now you're selling, you made $9,000 out of 10, right? Which means that your max break even for lead is $90 lead, okay? So this is not like an incremental proof. This is massive, okay? And so mind you, this break even cost doesn't even take into account. Recurring revenue, paid in fulls or product sales. Now, if we do take this into account, the contract becomes even larger, right? So look at this. So this is an optimized model. This is how you actually should be running this, all right? So you spend the same thousand dollars, you get 100 leads, all right? 70 schedule, 35 show, 15 no-sale sales, which is basically where someone comes in, and if they choose not to work with you, you actually give them a templated version for free and then you upsell their product later. All right, so 20 close at 500, 90% profit margin, challenge. You get 20 people, again, right now at 1,000, this is the same as before, all right? Now, of those 20 people, you sell them all supplements who purchased, and that's at a 42% margin, which adds another 1680 in terms of profit in your pocket, all right? On top of that, you sell no-sale sales. So these numbers are taken from our statistical averages that we're seeing right now. So you'll sell 10 out of the 15 people, under a $315 average, take it at 42% margin again. It's another 1323. And then out of all the clients you have, you'll typically get 20% to a paid-in-full option, which, by the way, for online is actually higher than you're selling your commoditized group training for. So you sell $3,000 for your paid-in-full is $18,000. All right, for just 20% of those people taking that offer, all right? And then on top of that, the other 80% of people, remember, because only 20% took the paid-in-full, 80% are still left, you convert those people into recurring. And so the remaining of the first EFT is $200 a month times 20, times 90% margin. You'll get a $3,600 extra, and that is now recurring revenue, okay? Now, total profit from this first run is $33,603 with an optimized model. And so that breaks down to $336 per lead break-even, which in any local market, you will be able to market until you're dead, right? Because this is weight loss. So this is a mass appeal offer. And if you can spend that much on traffic, you can, you change the game, right? And so this is no longer an incremental improvement. We're not taking from $20 lead to $30 lead, or $20 to $25 lead. This is $20 lead to $336 lead. It's insane, right? It's a new model. And so online training is the missing piece to your gym right now, or your fitness business, right? It's the key to future-proofing your business, right? And so no longer does the seven-figure gym owner mean make you $200,000 a year net, or $250,000 a year net on a million bucks, right? With $800,000 a year in overhead. But instead, it's looking at a potential $700,000 a year plus an income-earning opportunity, because it's the new game. It's a new way of doing it, because it's all profit, right? It's a better model. And so here's the four secrets to adding online to your current gym, all right? I'm just going to run through these quickly, because if you're like me, you don't got a lot of time. So number one, why break and mortar? Group training industry is doomed to commodization, so I'll explain that. Number two, why getting online clients is even easier than in person, and why people are lining up to pay more for online training than working out at your gym. No shit. Three, getting online clients great results actually takes less time, money, and effort than in person, and will reduce your overhead as well. And then four, how to transform last month's growth sales into your next month's profit in as little as 30 days, without a dollar out of pocket. So I'm going to show you how to do it. So why the break and mortar training industry is doomed to commodization? So to understand the future and support and understand what got us here, all right? So this is the fitness industry, kind of the evolution over time. So 1970s, 2000, facility usage, gyms development, that was kind of the development of the fitness industry. So gyms like LA Fitness, 24-hour fitness, things like that, big box gyms that were based off of facility leasage, meaning you would essentially micro-lease the facility for use, and there's thousands and thousands of people who did that, and that's pretty much what they did, and they sold 101PT on top of that, and that kind of came later, because between 2000 and 2006, 2001, 2006, the heyday of personal training kind of was born. That's where everybody who was personal trainer was making six figures a year, it was awesome. And then the boom of the, or so the crash in the economy happened, right? So that everyone couldn't afford the personal training anymore, and so they went outside to outdoor boot camps, right? And that's kind of the birth of group training as it appeared. And then 2011 to 2015, the economy started getting better, and then indoor group training started, and that's where boxes started showing up, boutique training studios, et cetera, right? And then 2015 to the present is when the transformation center model was born, and that's kind of the model that we have been running up to this point. It increases the average ticket and lifetime value by two to three X, and that's the model that we've been able to use to out-compete. Now, in the present, where we're at now, is hybrid. All right, and so with each of these evolutions, the price of group training has continued to drop, right? Because consumers value workouts less and less, and they're becoming increasingly commoditized. And so to prove my point, let me ask you a question. What separates our group training sessions from the group training sessions of these guys, like Equinox, right? Or Lifetime? Most of these guys charge $59 to $99 a month to start, right? They have beautiful bathrooms, locker rooms, amenities, pools, racquetball courts, basketball courts, equal or better trainers, because they pay more and they have access to the exact same pool of talent as we do, right? They have newer equipment, they have better parking, and they have a full gym that they can use, their clients can use when they're not doing group classes, right? And the price will only continue to go one way, down. And so how do we stay in business, right? By playing the new game. And so this came to me when we created the retention system. So this is a system that we know retains customers in large groups. Like, we've done this over and over again. This is how you go from double digit turn rates month over month to sub 3%, like this is the system, right? And so we had to create an entire coaching accountability system where we retained customers at $200 a month. Why did we have to do that? Because they didn't value workouts alone, right? And so then it dawned on me. The service that they actually valued cost me close to nothing to fulfill. And the majority of my overhead was spent providing a service that they didn't actually value, the workouts. And so I was like, hmm, why don't we just give them what they want, one-on-one attention and coaching, and then get rid of the service that they don't value, which cost me all the money. And so then we tried it. And it worked a lot, which leads to the second discovery we had, which is why getting online clients even easier than in person and how people are already lining up to pay more for online training than working out in our gyms, OK? Carlos went three for three again. And then Ryan's like posting every single one of the sales that he got, so there's a whole bunch of sales, right? But why is this working so well? But many just don't want to pay PT prices for it. And now we can finally give them what they want and give them what they need. And we can either let these guys eat our lunch or be ahead and ride the next wave, all right? And so this is going to be the fitness gold rush of 2020 just like the Facebook marketing was six years ago. All right, so here's the quick recap. Group models commoditized and has doomed the race to the bottom. It's easy to get online clients with a hybrid gym. It's easier to sell since customers prefer staying at home which is because of the boom of at-home fitness services. They pay more for it because consumers prefer one-on-one attention to group, right? And pivoting to a hybrid model will be like getting on Facebook marketing six years ago, okay? So we covered one and two. Number three is getting online clients great results takes less time, money and effort than in person and will reduce your overhead. So number three, I will be the first to admit the current gym model exposes you to risk, okay? And as you scale, you incur more risk for overhead, right? And mine might do. This is common in virtually every business. Like I have more overhead than you do because my business bigger. Like that's part of how it works, okay? Now some of you may have already experienced this. As you scale, you need to hire more trainers, more admin staff, more challenge manager, retention coordinator, sales staff, et cetera, right? And all your profit goes to payroll, sad face, right? And it can feel like the end is never in sight, right? One why make enough just to keep it, right? And so even if you make 50,000 a month and if you have 40,000 month in expenses, you're still exposed to a great deal of risk, right? Especially if you're still reliant on front and cash flow to be profitable, making you reliant on the big blue box of black magic. Because if you even hiccup once, your profit vanishes, right? And if your anxiety goes up because even with a $500,000 in your business, you're exposed to 400,000 year in expenses, you're only two bed months away from losing a year for the profit and that's assuming you only live on $0, right? So some of you experienced this, right? I'm just joking, we all have, right? And until now there just wasn't a better model that anyone knew that was better, right? That could scale and they had better than 20, 30% margins for larger training in a brick and mortar location, right? But now there is a low cost option to provide high value service, right? So we already established that people want remote training and they're willing to pay for it. So how much does it cost us to fulfill, right? The answer is not that much. So cost of online remote fulfillment is about 60 minutes a month, so 15 minutes a week to check in and provide feedback and accountability, which you can either do yourself and have zero cost on just your time, or you can pay a trainer to do it for 20% of the revenue, all right? So imagine if you needed 80% margin instead of a 10 or 20% margin from selling services and when EFTs increase, so too did your profit and if they decreased, like so did your overhead, right? So it's a lower risk, you have less fixed costs, right? It's variable costs and you keep your margin constant, all right? But then the question is like, okay, I get it, it costs me not a lot of money, but do the clients get good results? Of course they do, right? Since we all know that nutrition is 90% of the battle for GenPop anyways, and that's who most of your clients are, right? And allowing them to experiment with different types of training allows them the opportunity to find something they can actually stick with forever. That's the point, it's sustainability, right? And so if you can say this with me, retention, right? If we can personalize things so they actually stick with it so that it's sustainable, right? It might not be working at your gym every day, that might not actually be the most sustainable thing for them, finding what is makes them retain longer, right? And value no longer means people trading time for money, whether it's you or trainers, right? And so this is just a quote from Dr. Katchy who runs a seven figure online coaching business, trained Olympians, he consulted with precision nutrition to write their book, to do their certification, all that stuff. And if you're talking, like he says, if you're taking more than 60 minutes per month on an online client than your fuck amount and you're not targeting your actions, okay? And this is him, and he's coaching pros and Olympians, right? Like real competitors. So we've established that they get great sustainable results and it doesn't cost much time on your effort. What does this mean for the business, right? Becomes low risk, that's what happens, right? And so the gyms have fully implemented this, we're able to drop the total overhead from like 25 to 30,000 a month under 10, right? Which means low leads month doesn't wipe out your bank account, a bad sales month doesn't mean you stress over making payroll, making rent. A low show rate day doesn't mean you have to massively discount your paid and fools and sell your soul to survive, which means your business becomes resilient, and you get peace of mind. Knowing, not wondering, that you have more than enough profit to pay for your bills three times over every single month. So right now, if you could add online training to your business and make the same money you do now, both 80% margins, would you do it? If you could swap your bootcamp revenue or your large group revenue for online training revenue and your only overhead was rent, would you? If you answered yes, then you're smart because this is the lowest risk, highest return business decision, right? And those do not come by often, right? Typically, opportunities to increase profit, increase revenue potential, increase client personalization, match consumer desires, and increase trainer pay, usually increase risk. Except this one decreases risk, because it decreases payroll, it decreases liability, decreases churn, decreases sales obstacles, right? And so the only thing that stops anyone from making this change is fear, except the fear of change comes from the business overhead that would be removed by making the change. Which means if anything, the fear should be of not making the change, because the only thing that you're worried about having happened is paying for something that will no longer persist after you make that change, right? And on top of this, you can finally decrease the complexity of your business, which means less people, and only keep the people you want and pay them more, right? This is making sense, more profit, less risk, limitless earning potential, and that's why Secret3, getting all my clients great results, take less time, money, and effort than in person, and reduce your overhead is true. Which brings me to how to transform your last month's gross sales into your next month's profit in those 30 days without a dollar out of pocket. So we're gonna go do a mental exercise. Think about exactly what you grossed in revenue and sales last month. Picture the number in your head. Now think about the check you're going to write yourself to take on this month. What if those two numbers were the same? What would that be worth to you? A lot, or maybe precisely $36,793, right? Some of you may have heard of the Wedding Cake Gym Profit Model, and it's the model that our gymlers used to get $100,000 a month, right? So understand how this works, there's two major pieces. Tasty Dispatch Number One, which is the icing, right? So the icing represents the front end cash of a business, right, the cake has icing and cake, all right? So icing is the front end cash, which hopefully you see as incredibly important because it allows you to acquire customers profitably, right? You make money in the front end, which allows you to put more money into marketing so that you can acquire more customers, right? It's a virtuous cycle. But a gate can't be made of entirely icing, right? Because on a hot day, when summer comes, which it does, right? You need to also have structure and consistency where the cake melts, right? So that it can hold its shape when the pressure gets turned on, AKA the business is consistently profitable. Which brings me to Tasty Dispatch Number Two, which is the cakeiness, right? And that represents the back end cash flow of a gym business, AKA recurring EFT. And so this gives the business consistent cash flow regardless of the seasons and conditions, and this may seem obvious, but I want to make sure that we have our definitions clear. This is the model that I explain and is the model that has gotten countless gym alerts over a seven figure run remark, right? And here's a visual of the $100,000 a month weighting kick model, right? So this is the old wedding kick model. And so people make $20,000 a month on the front end, $25,000 a month on the back, so that's $45,000 for their base level for a large group. They're making $10,000 a month on semi-private front ends, which is 12 week programs. They usually sell them for $2,500, so it's one person a week. And then on the back end, that amounts to about $30,000 a month in semi-private training, so it's $40,000. All right? Next, your front end cash of internal places, $5,000 a month, and those funnel into semi-private, which has already been accounted for, so it's another $5,000 a month. And then supplement sales, very realistically, you get $5,000 a month on the front, and then $5,000 in recurring, so about $10,000 a month total. All right? And so that's how you get to a $100,000 a month gym. That's it, we've been running this over and over again, and it works, right? And up until now, this has been the single best model on the market, right? We've got tons and tons of success stories. I could read you all of them. Lots and lots and lots and lots of them. Lots and lots and lots and lots of them. Lots and lots and lots and lots of them, right? And forge a lot of the gym works that some of you guys might know. Time Miller went from $25,000 a month to $100,000 a month, plus a month, right? Rob Group went from $35,000 a month to $100,000 a month. Joe Hueber went from $45,000 a month to $150,000 a month. Juan Lobano went from $16,000 a month to $90,000 a month. Caleb and Erica went from $30,000 a month to $86,000 a month. Right? And so, we're back to the $15,000 to $91,000. Jenny and Brian's winning from $25,000 a month to $93,000 a month. Thorough from $16,000 to $90,000 a month. Vinay went from $35,000 to $91,000 a month. At two gyms, not one, but two locations, and I can keep going, right? And that amount of change to the industry. But this new hybrid model will transform the future of the fitness industry, right? And so if you look at the difference between the old model and the new model, the old models, the one with the red box in the corner, and the new model is one with the green box. And so, everything is actually more or less the same between our original model, which has been immensely successful, and the new one. It's just that we're switching the core offering. So instead of being a large group, the core offering is actually hybrid or remote coaching. Right? And so that's the only real change. You still have a value ladder, you still have other ascensions, other things that you're gonna sell people in order to provide more value and make more profit. But that's the only real switch that's happening here. And the beauty of this model is that you can use it no matter what type of gym you have, right? Whether you're a bootcamp, summer private, affiliate box, hybrid facility, K-Boxing Studio, BJJ, Dance, Yoga, Disney theme, Pirates of Fitness, whatever, right? No matter what size of gym you have, no matter how many members you have, it still works, right? And so in the next video, I'm gonna show you the real world number examples of different ways of implementing hybrid depending on where you're at, what type of fitness business you have, and just some examples of people who've crushed it so that you can kind of pick the one that best matches kind of where you're at so that you can see how you can implement it and ultimately make more and serve more.
